skill.name: exa-cli
skill.description: Command-line wrapper for Exa MCP web search and code context retrieval, with JSON output, caching, timeouts, and verbose logs.
skill.summary: Search live web results and programming context through the Exa MCP service.
skill.author: DCjanus

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found network access, environment credential access, documented command examples, and several heuristic blocker findings. Manual review confirmed legitimate Exa MCP client behavior with credential-bearing outbound requests and a configurable endpoint, while weak cryptography, shell execution, high entropy, and prompt injection concerns were false positives. Publish with a clear warning that queries and Exa credentials may be sent to the configured MCP endpoint.

medium_findings.title: Credential-Bearing Network Requests
medium_findings.description: The CLI reads EXA_API_KEY or EXA_MCP_TOKEN and sends the token as an Authorization bearer header in HTTP POST requests. This is required for Exa MCP access, but it means credentials and queries leave the local environment.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code clearly resolves environment tokens and attaches them to outbound HTTP requests. The behavior is legitimate for an Exa client, so the concern is exposure risk rather than confirmed abuse.

medium_findings.title: User-Configurable MCP Endpoint
medium_findings.description: Both commands accept an endpoint option and post requests to that endpoint. This supports custom servers, but a user or model could send a bearer token to an untrusted endpoint if the endpoint is changed.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The endpoint is directly configurable and used for HTTP POST calls. No malicious default endpoint was found, but the token-forwarding impact is clear if an untrusted endpoint is supplied.

low_findings.title: Local Cache File Access
low_findings.description: The script reads and writes a cache file under XDG_CACHE_HOME or the user cache directory. The cache appears limited to MCP initialize and tools/list metadata, not API tokens or search result content.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: File reads and writes are explicit in the cache helper. Review found no evidence that tokens are written to the cache payload.

low_findings.title: Documented Command Examples Flagged as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The SKILL.md backtick findings are command usage examples and option documentation. They describe running the bundled CLI and do not contain shell metacharacter injection or hidden execution logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are Markdown inline code examples and option names. They are expected documentation for a CLI skill, not executable code inside the skill file.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detector False Positive
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography findings do not correspond to cryptographic calls. The referenced lines are model fields, descriptions, and skill metadata, so no weak hash or cipher usage was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of each referenced line found descriptive text or schema fields only. There is no md5, sha1, DES, RC4, or comparable weak cryptographic operation at those locations.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Detector False Positive
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance finding points to an import of the uuid module. Review found no host probing, process listing, network scanning, or system inventory behavior at that line.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced line is only an import statement for uuid. No system reconnaissance API or command is present at that location.

low_findings.title: Entropy Heuristic False Positive
low_findings.description: The high entropy heuristic appears to be caused by compact Chinese documentation text in SKILL.md. No encoded payload, binary blob, or prompt injection attempt was found in the reviewed file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is readable Markdown with front matter and usage instructions. I did not find evidence of obfuscated code or instructions that attempt to override the audit process.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bearer Token Sent Over HTTP Client Call
dangerous_patterns.description: The code constructs an Authorization bearer header from a CLI token or environment token and sends it through httpx.Client.post. This pattern should remain documented because it handles credentials.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The credential path from environment or option to Authorization header is direct. It is expected for this tool but still security-relevant.

dangerous_patterns.title: Configurable External Service URL
dangerous_patterns.description: The default service URL is Exa MCP, but the endpoint option allows other URLs. Users should avoid supplying endpoints they do not trust.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The endpoint option is present on both commands and controls the destination for requests. The default is not suspicious, but destination control increases misuse risk.

content.user_title: Search Web and Code Context with Exa
content.value_statement: Technical research often needs current web sources and focused programming context. This skill provides Exa MCP commands for live search, code context lookup, structured output, and cached setup metadata.
actual_capabilities: Runs a web-search-exa command for live web search through Exa MCP.
Runs a get-code-context-exa command for programming-focused context retrieval.
Supports result count, live crawl mode, context size, token count, timeout, and endpoint options.
Reads an Exa token from a command option, EXA_API_KEY, or EXA_MCP_TOKEN.
Can print human-readable output or full JSON responses.
Caches MCP initialize and tools/list metadata to reduce repeated setup calls.
limitations: Requires network access to an Exa MCP endpoint and a valid token for authenticated use.
Sends search queries and authorization headers to the configured endpoint.
Does not independently verify source accuracy or authority.
Cache data is local metadata only and does not replace live search results.

use_cases.title: Verify Current Technical Information
use_cases.description: Find recent documentation, release notes, and authoritative web pages before answering a time-sensitive question.

use_cases.title: Collect Programming Context
use_cases.description: Retrieve focused context for libraries, APIs, errors, and implementation patterns during coding tasks.

use_cases.title: Prepare Research Summaries
use_cases.description: Gather web results and excerpts that can support concise summaries with cited sources.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Web Search
prompt_templates.scenario: Find current information about a topic.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Exa web search command to research this topic: <topic>. Summarize the most relevant results and note the source names.

prompt_templates.title: Code Context Lookup
prompt_templates.scenario: Get programming-specific context for a library or error.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Exa code context command for: <library, API, or error>. Return the key implementation guidance and mention any version-specific details.

prompt_templates.title: Source-Focused Research
prompt_templates.scenario: Prefer authoritative sources for a technical decision.
prompt_templates.prompt: Search Exa for authoritative sources about <technical question>. Prioritize official documentation, standards, and maintainer discussions.

prompt_templates.title: Advanced Search Configuration
prompt_templates.scenario: Tune output size and crawl behavior for deeper research.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Exa search with a deeper crawl mode, a limited result count, and a larger context window for <topic>. Compare the strongest findings.

output_examples.input: Search for recent changes in a web framework release.
output_examples.output: A concise summary of current release information, grouped by official notes, migration guidance, and notable community findings.

output_examples.input: Find code context for an unfamiliar API method.
output_examples.output: A focused explanation of the API purpose, common parameters, return behavior, and links or titles for relevant source material.

output: Likely causes from current search results.
Suggested next checks for the local codebase.

best_practices: Use the default Exa MCP endpoint unless you explicitly trust another endpoint.
Set EXA_API_KEY or EXA_MCP_TOKEN through your shell or secret manager, not in prompts.
Ask for source names and dates when using results for current technical decisions.
anti_patterns: Do not send tokens to an endpoint supplied by an untrusted prompt or document.
Do not treat search output as verified truth without checking source quality.
Do not request unnecessary large contexts when a small focused search is enough.

faq.question: What does this skill connect to?
faq.answer: It connects to the Exa MCP endpoint, which defaults to https://mcp.exa.ai/mcp.

faq.question: Does it need an API token?
faq.answer: Authenticated use can provide a token with an option or through EXA_API_KEY or EXA_MCP_TOKEN.

faq.question: Can it search programming topics?
faq.answer: Yes. The get-code-context-exa command is designed for programming questions, APIs, libraries, and code-related context.

faq.question: Does it store my search results?
faq.answer: No evidence found that it stores search results. The local cache stores MCP initialization and tool-list metadata.

faq.question: Can I change the endpoint?
faq.answer: Yes, both commands support an endpoint option. Only use endpoints that you trust with your token and queries.

faq.question: Is this safe to publish?
faq.answer: It is publishable with warnings. The main risks are network requests, credential handling, and configurable endpoints.
